[!IMPORTANT] Form Integration: If your builder schema involves input fields, you MUST wrap the $ui->render() call within a standard HTML <form> tag and provide a security Token: echo '<form method="POST">'; $ui->render(); echo '<input type="hidden" name="token" value="'.TOKEN.'">'; echo '</form>';

[!TIP] Component Reusability: Developers are encouraged to break large schemas into smaller, manageable partials. You can merge different arrays before passing them to the constructor for a highly modularized UI architecture.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Token Class — Securing UiBuilder forms against CSRF.
  • OptionsBuilder Class — Building specialized settings panels.
  • Dashboard Guide — How core GeniXCMS screens utilize this engine.

docs.title: File Ingestion & Upload Class
docs.text: The Upload class is the primary interface for handling secure file ingestion into GeniXCMS. It provides an abstraction layer over PHP's native move_uploaded_file system, adding critical features like strict extension filtering, automatic filename sanitization, and unique storage path generation.

docs.title: Upload::go(string $name, ...)
docs.text:
Processes a single file upload from an HTML form.

Parameter Type Default Description $name string Required The name attribute of the <input type="file"> field. $path string Required Destination folder relative to GX_PATH (e.g., assets/uploads/). $allowed array [] List of permitted extensions (e.g., ['jpg', 'png']). $unique bool false If true, creates a cryptographically unique filename.

Returns: array (Result/Metadata) or string (Error message if failed).

// Example: Processing a site logo upload
$valid_ext = ['jpg', 'png', 'svg', 'webp'];
$result = Upload::go('site_logo', 'assets/images/', $valid_ext, true);

if (isset($result['error'])) {
    System::alert(['alertDanger' => [$result['error']]]);
} else {
    $logo_url = $result['fileurl'];
}

docs.title: 📦 Result Metadata Schema
docs.text:
Upon a successful upload, the engine returns a detailed metadata object for immediate use or database persistence:

  • filename: The final, sanitized name of the file (including any unique prefixes).
  • filesize: The exact volume in bytes.
  • path: The relative storage path (e.g., assets/images/logo.png).
  • filepath: The absolute server filesystem path.
  • fileurl: The derived public-facing URL resolved via the Url Class.

docs.title: 🛡️ Integrated Security Features
docs.text:
Protection is baked into every phase of the upload lifecycle:

  1. Strict Whitelisting: The system ignores MIME types provided by the client and relies exclusively on your provided $allowed extensions list.
  2. Naming Sanitization: Filenames are processed through Typo::slugify() and Typo::cleanX() to remove spaces, special characters, and potential script injection points.
  3. Unique Collisions: Enabling $unique prevents overwriting existing files and makes user-uploaded assets unguessable for external scanners.
  4. Directory Integrity: The system automatically attempts to create the target $path if it does not already exist.

[!IMPORTANT] Server Limits: File uploads are subject to your PHP upload_max_filesize and post_max_size configuration. If a large file disappears during upload, check these settings in your php.ini.

[!CAUTION] Extension Spoofing: Always include your own server-side validation. While the GeniXCMS Upload class checks extensions, for high-security environments, consider the Files Class to verify actual file headers and content integrity.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Files Class — Advanced media header and checksum verification.
  • Image Class — Optimizing and resizing images after upload.
  • Url Class — How the public upload URL is calculated.

docs.title: Permalink Resolution & URL Class
docs.text: The Url class is the primary routing abstraction for GeniXCMS. It provides a centralized, future-proof suite of methods to generate absolute URLs for every content entity — including posts, pages, categories, tags, and theme-specific assets — while automatically handling the complexity of Smart URL (Permalinks) logic.

docs.title: ⚡ Core Permalink Generators
docs.text:
These methods are the essential toolkit for theme developers, ensuring that your links remain valid regardless of the system's global URL configuration.

Method Role Return Type Url::post($id) Individual Article string (The full permalink to a blog post). Url::page($id) Static Content string (The full permalink to a static page). Url::cat($id) Category Archive string (The full permalink to a taxonomy listing). Url::tag($slug) Tag Archive string (The full permalink to a tag collection). Url::search() Search Results string (The base search endpoint).

docs.title: Url::theme()
docs.text:
Resolves the absolute public URL to your active theme's directory.

Best Use Case: Correctly loading CSS, JavaScript, and Images within your header.php. 

<link rel="stylesheet" href="{Url::theme()}css/style.css">

docs.title: Url::thumb(string $src, ...)
docs.text:
Generates the specific URL pattern for the system's Image Class thumbnail engine.

// Construct a 300px square thumbnail URL
$thumb_url = Url::thumb($image_path, 'square', 300);

docs.title: Url::breadcrumbs(array $data)
docs.text:
Generates Bootstrap-compliant HTML for site Breadcrumbs.

  • Process: Intelligently traces the path from the homepage to the current category or page based on the $data context array.
  • Output: Returns a complete <ol class="breadcrumb"> structure.

docs.title: 🏹 Syndication & Discovery
docs.text:
• Url::sitemap($file): Resolves the location of the master or type-specific XML Sitemap.
  • Url::rss(): Resolves the primary content syndication endpoint.

[!IMPORTANT] Smart URL Transparency: You should never hardcode index.php?post=12 in your themes. By using Url::post(12), GeniXCMS will automatically serve the clean, professional path (e.g., /my-new-post.html) if Smart URLs are enabled in the dashboard.

[!TIP] Theme Asset Loading: Always combine Url::theme() with specific folder paths to ensure your theme remains portable across local development and production environments.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Router Class — How the system resolves these generated URLs back to controllers.
  • Image Class — The service behind the Url::thumb() method.
  • Permalink Settings — Configuring the Smart URL engine.

docs.title: Identity & Authentication Class
docs.text: The User class is the central security and identity management engine for GeniXCMS. It handles the entire user lifecycle, from secure registration and role-based access control (RBAC) to profile management, session persistence, and multi-level administrative permissions.

docs.title: 🔐 Authentication & Session Security
docs.text: GeniXCMS implements a robust session-based security layer linked to the Session Class.

docs.title: User::secure()
docs.text: The primary middleware for protected pages. It verifies the active user session; if no session is detected, it automatically redirects the visitor to the login page with an encoded backto return URL.

docs.title: User::isLoggedin()
docs.text: Returns a bool indicating if the visitor is currently authenticated within the system.

docs.title: 🛡️ Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
docs.text:
GeniXCMS uses a tiered numeric level system. Access is granted if the user's level is equal to or lower (smaller numeric value) than the requirement.

Level Role Description 0 Administrator Full system-wide orchestration. 1 Supervisor Site-wide management without kernel access. 2 Editor Content curation and comment moderation. 3 Author Content creation and personal post management. 6 General Member Default level for registered subscribers.

docs.title: User::access(string $lvl)
docs.text:
Checks if the current user meets the specified clearance level.

// Only allow Editors and above
if (User::access('2')) {
    echo "Access granted to editorial tools.";
}

docs.title: User::create(array $data) / User::update(array $data)
docs.text:
Standardized methods for committing user records to the database.

  • Data Encryption: Passwords are automatically hashed using the current system-wide encryption algorithms (e.g., PASSWORD_BCRYPT).
  • Validation: Ensures unique User IDs and Email addresses during the creation process.

docs.title: 📂 Identity Retrieval Helpers
docs.text:
Quickly resolve specific user attributes using their unique ID or Username.

  • User::avatar($id): Resolves the user's profile image or Gravatar based on site settings.
  • User::email($id): Fetches the unique contact address for a specific user ID.
  • User::group($id): Returns the plain-text role name associated with the user's numeric level.

[!IMPORTANT] Password Security: Never attempt to manually hash passwords using md5() or sha1(). Always use the User::create() and User::update() methods, which leverage modern, salt-aware PHP password hashing functions.

[!TIP] Custom Redirects: The User::secure() method is typically called at the top of protected controller files to prevent unauthorized execution of your business logic.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Session Class — How user states are persistently stored.
  • Acl Class — Managing fine-grained permission matrices.
  • Users Guide — Managing accounts from the dashboard.

docs.title: Vendor Management Class
docs.text: The Vendor class is the central orchestrator for third-party libraries within GeniXCMS. It primarily manages the integration of Composer-based packages and legacy non-PSR libraries, ensuring that external dependencies are available, secured, and properly mapped to both internal paths and public URLs.

docs.title: Vendor::autoload()
docs.text:
This method manually triggers the inclusion of the main Composer autoload.php. It is called internally by the system kernel to prepare the environment for third-party classes.

// Typically called once during system boot
Vendor::autoload();

docs.title: Vendor::loadonce(string $library_path)
docs.text:
For libraries that do not support PSR-4 autoloading or aren't managed via Composer's main manifest, use loadonce() to include them safely from the inc/lib/Vendor/ directory.

// Example: Manually loading a specific library component
Vendor::loadonce('tcpdf/tcpdf.php');

docs.title: 🔗 Path & URL Resolution
docs.text:
Managing assets from vendor packages requires consistent URL and path generation.

Method Return Value Common Use Case Vendor::url() string Including vendor CSS/JS in themes or modules (e.g., Bootstrap, FontAwesome). Vendor::path() string Reading library configuration files or checking for file existence on the server.

docs.title: 📦 Integrated Core Libraries
docs.text: GeniXCMS comes pre-packaged with several industry-standard libraries managed through the Vendor layer:

docs.title: 🛡️ Security & Content
docs.text:
• HTMLPurifier: The gold standard for sanitizing user-generated HTML and preventing XSS.
  • Latte: The primary templating engine featuring automated contextual escaping.
  • Summernote: Lightweight WYSIWYG editor for the content dashboard.

docs.title: 📧 Communication & Media
docs.text:
• PHPMailer: Enterprise-grade email transmission engine.
  • Intervention Image: High-level API for dynamic image processing, cropping, and WebP conversion.
  • GuzzleHttp: Robust HTTP client for handling remote API requests.

[!TIP] Extending Vendors: If you are adding a new library via Composer, make sure to run composer update in the inc/lib/ directory to update the vendor autoloader, then call Vendor::autoload() within your module or theme logic.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Security Class — How vendors like HTMLPurifier are used for data sanitization.
  • Vite Helper — Managing modern frontend assets alongside legacy vendor libraries.

docs.title: Vite Helper Class
docs.text: The Vite class is a specialized utility designed to bridge the gap between GeniXCMS and the Vite asset bundling ecosystem. It enables high-performance frontend development workflows, including Hot Module Replacement (HMR) and automated production asset management.

docs.title: 🛠️ Configuration Defaults
docs.text: Key Default Value Description Dev Server http://localhost:5173 The default URL where the Vite development server runs. Main Entry assets/src/main.js The primary JavaScript file to be injected. Style Entry assets/src/style.scss The root style file (SCSS/CSS) for the theme.

docs.title: static Vite::isDev()
docs.text:
Determines the current operational mode of the asset pipeline.

  • Storage: Driven by the vite_dev_mode option in the database.
  • States: Returns true if set to on, otherwise false.
  • Primary Use: Used internally by client() to switch between HMR and Manifest loading.

docs.title: static Vite::client($entry = 'assets/src/main.js')
docs.text: The primary injector for your theme's head or footer. It intelligently handles asset loading based on your environment.

docs.title: 🔄 Development Mode (HMR)
docs.text: When active, it injects the following into your HTML: 1. Vite Client: The @vite/client runtime for real-time updates. 2. Entry Point: Your source file (e.g., main.js) served directly from the dev server.

docs.title: 🚀 Production Mode (Manifest)
docs.text: When inactive, it follows this sequence: 1. Lookup: Accesses assets/dist/manifest.json. 2. JS Injection: Loads the versioned/hashed JavaScript bundle. 3. CSS Injection: Automatically discovers and injects any associated CSS chunks mapped to that entry point.

docs.title: static Vite::setDevServer($url)
docs.text:
Overrides the default development server address. Use this if you are running Vite on a custom port or within a specific container.

// Example: Custom port configuration
Vite::setDevServer('http://localhost:3000');

docs.title: 📂 Implementation in Themes
docs.text:
Integrating Vite into your theme requires only a single call in your header.php or footer.php:

// Load assets dynamically
Vite::client('assets/src/main.js');

[!TIP] Performance First: In production mode, the Vite class ensures that only the necessary versioned assets are loaded, preventing cache-busting issues and reducing page weight.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• How to Use Vite — Step-by-step guide for setting up your first Vite theme.
  • Options Class — Learn how to toggle vite_dev_mode via code.

docs.title: Aspect-Based Widget & Sidebar Class
docs.text: The Widget class is the primary engine for rendering dynamic, reusable content blocks (Widgets) across various theme locations. It provides a standardized framework for both core system widgets (e.g., Recent Posts, Tag Cloud) and custom module-driven components, ensuring a consistent visual architecture throughout your platform.

docs.title: ⚡ Active Rendering Logic
docs.text: GeniXCMS widgets are location-aware. They are retrieved and rendered based on their assigned region in the admin dashboard.

docs.title: Widget::show(string $location)
docs.text:
The primary entry point for theme integration.

  • Process: Fetches all active records assigned to the specified $location (e.g., sidebar, footer-1), sorts them by their manual priority, and returns the aggregated HTML.
  • Implementation: Typically called within a theme's sidebar.php or footer.php.
{* In your theme's sidebar.tpl *}
<aside class="col-md-4 main-sidebar">
    {Widget::show('sidebar')|noescape}
</aside>

docs.title: 🏗️ Supported Content Types
docs.text:
The Widget engine automatically resolves various content sources into standardized HTML modules:

Type Description Recent Posts Automatically fetches the 5 most recent articles with thumbnails. Recent Comments Lists newest reader interactions for social proof. Tag Cloud Injects a frequency-weighted list of site-wide tags. Static Content Renders raw HTML or text provided via the admin editor. Module Hook Dynamically triggers a specified Hook to pull data from a third-party module.

docs.title: 🎨 Professional UI Blueprint
docs.text:
Every widget is wrapped in a standardized, Bootstrap-compliant container to ensure design consistency across all themes.

<div class="widget-box mb-4" id="widget-12">
    <div class="widget-header">
        <h3 class="widget-title h5 fw-bold m-0 text-uppercase">
            <i class="bi bi-star-fill me-2"></i> Widget Title
        </h3>
    </div>
    <div class="widget-body">
        <!-- Widget Dynamic Content ... -->
    </div>
</div>

docs.title: 🏹 Management Workflow
docs.text:
• Drag & Drop: Reorder widgets in the dashboard under Appearance > Widgets.
  • Location Mapping: Assign widgets to specific theme slots (e.g., Header, Main Sidebar, Post Footer).
  • Configuration: Edit the internal title and content (plain text or HTML) for each instance.

[!TIP] Custom Module Widgets: To create a widget for your module, use the Custom Module type and specify a unique hook name. Then, in your module's function.php, use Hooks::attach('your_hook', 'your_render_function') to inject your own HTML.

[!IMPORTANT] Styling Consistency: Ensure your theme's CSS targets the .widget-box, .widget-header, and .widget-body classes to maintain a premium, unified aesthetic for the entire sidebar subsystem.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks Class — How module-driven widgets are registered.
  • Theme Class — Integrating widget locations into your templates.
  • Widgets Guide — Managing your site's sidebars in the dashboard.

docs.title: Bot Protection (reCAPTCHA) Class
docs.text: The Xaptcha class is a specialized security wrapper for Google reCAPTCHA v2. It provides GeniXCMS with a streamlined, high-level interface for implementing bot protection across sensitive forms — including comments, registrations, and administrative logins — ensuring your site remains secure from automated spam and brute-force attacks.

docs.title: ⚡ Global Configuration
docs.text:
CAPTCHA policies are managed centrally via the Settings > General dashboard.

Parameter Identifier Description Enable google_captcha_enable Global toggle (on/off) for the entire system. Site Key google_captcha_sitekey Your public public-facing key from Google Console. Secret Key google_captcha_secret Your private server-side verification key. Language google_captcha_lang The ISO language code for the widget (e.g., en, id).

docs.title: Xaptcha::html()
docs.text:
Generates and injects the complete reCAPTCHA widget logic.

  • Process: Automatically includes the Google JavaScript library from the global CDN and renders the <div class="g-recaptcha"> container with your configured Site Key.
  • Safety: Returns an empty string if CAPTCHA is disabled, allowing for transparent theme integration.

docs.title: Xaptcha::verify(string $response)
docs.text:
Performs a secure server-to-server handshake with Google's API to validate the submission.

// Typically executed within a form controller
$token = $_POST['g-recaptcha-response'];
if (Xaptcha::verify($token)) {
    // Human visitor verified
}

docs.title: 📝 Implementation Pattern
docs.text: Use the following pattern to secure your module's forms:

docs.title: Frontend (Latte / PHP Template)
docs.text:
{* In your theme's register.tpl *}
<div class="form-group mb-3">
    {if Xaptcha::isEnable()}
        {Xaptcha::html()|noescape}
    {/if}
</div>

docs.title: Backend (PHP Controller)
docs.text:
if (Xaptcha::isEnable()) {
    $captcha = $_POST['g-recaptcha-response'] ?? '';
    if (!Xaptcha::verify($captcha)) {
        System::alert(['alertDanger' => [_('Bot detected! Please complete the CAPTCHA.')]]);
        return;
    }
}

[!CAUTION] API Key Mismatch: If you see "Error: Invalid site key" in the widget, ensure that the domain name in your Google Cloud Console exactly matches your Site::$url.

[!IMPORTANT] Privacy Compliance: Google reCAPTCHA tracks user behavior to distinguish humans from bots. Ensure your site's Privacy Policy includes a notice regarding the use of this service to remain compliant with GDPR and other regulations.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Token Class — How CSRF protection works alongside CAPTCHA.
  • Security Settings — Managing site-wide protection layers.
  • General Settings Guide — Configuring your Google API keys.

docs.title: How to Create a Module
docs.text: This guide walks you through building a GeniXCMS module, either automatically using the CLI or manually from scratch.

docs.title: ⚡ Quick Start: Using the CLI
docs.text: The fastest way to scaffold a new module is by using the built-in genix console. This command automatically generates the required directory structure, the index.php entry point with metadata, and a base library class.

docs.title: Generate Scaffolding
docs.text:
In your project root, run:

php genix make:module my-new-plugin

docs.title: Activate the Module
docs.text:
Once generated, you can instantly activate it via the CLI:

php genix module:activate my-new-plugin

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
A module lives in inc/mod/{module-slug}/ and must contain at minimum:

inc/mod/my-module/
├── index.php           ← Required: Module entry point + metadata
├── options.php         ← Optional: Admin settings page
├── inc/
│   └── MyModule.lib.php  ← Main module class
└── layout/
    └── myview.php      ← Frontend template (Latte)

docs.title: Step 1: Create index.php (Entry Point)
docs.text:
The comment block at the top is parsed by the CMS to display the module in the admin panel.

<?php
/**
 * Name: My Module
 * Desc: A brief description of what this module does.
 * Version: 1.0.0
 * Build: 1.0.0
 * Developer: Your Name
 * URI: https://yoursite.com
 * License: MIT License
 * Icon: fa fa-cogs
 */

// Register a PSR-style autoloader for the module's own classes
function loadMyModuleLib($class_name)
{
    Mod::inc($class_name . ".lib", '', dirname(__FILE__) . "/inc/");
}
spl_autoload_register('loadMyModuleLib');

// Instantiate the main module class
new MyModule();

docs.title: Step 2: Create the Module Class (inc/MyModule.lib.php)
docs.text:
The module class constructor hooks into the CMS. The mod_control hook is the key action point for frontend pages.

<?php
class MyModule
{
    public function __construct()
    {
        // Attach to the frontend page hook
        Hooks::attach('mod_control', ['MyModule', 'myPage']);

        // Register this module's page in the Menus admin dropdown
        Mod::addMenuList(['myPage' => 'My Module Page']);

        // Optional: Attach to other hooks
        Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['MyModule', 'loadAssets']);
        Hooks::attach('admin_page_dashboard_action', ['MyModule', 'dashboardWidget']);
    }

    /**
     * Frontend page handler.
     * $data[0]['mod'] contains the module name from the URL.
     */
    public static function myPage($data)
    {
        // Only execute when this module's URL is active
        if ($data[0]['mod'] !== 'myPage') {
            return;
        }

        $page_data = [];

        // Handle form submission
        if (isset($_POST['submit'])) {
            $token = Typo::cleanX($_POST['token']);
            if (!Token::validate($token)) {
                $page_data['alertDanger'][] = _('Invalid security token.');
            } else {
                $message = Typo::cleanX($_POST['message']);
                // Process the form...
                $page_data['alertSuccess'][] = _('Submitted successfully!');
            }
        }

        // Render a view from the layout folder
        Mod::inc('myview', $page_data, realpath(__DIR__ . '/../layout/'));
    }

    public static function loadAssets()
    {
        echo '<script src="' . Site::$url . '/inc/mod/my-module/assets/app.js"></script>';
    }

    public static function dashboardWidget($data)
    {
        // Output a widget on the admin dashboard
        echo '<div class="col-md-4"><div class="info-box">My Module Stats</div></div>';
    }
}

docs.title: Step 3: Create a Frontend View (layout/myview.php)
docs.text:
Layout files use the Latte template engine.

<section class="container my-5">
    <h1>My Module Page</h1>

    {* Display alerts *}
    {if isset($alertSuccess)}
        <div class="alert alert-success">{$alertSuccess[0]}</div>
    {/if}

    {* Main form *}
    <form method="POST" action="">
        <input type="hidden" name="token" value="{$token}">

        <div class="form-group">
            <label>Your Message</label>
            <textarea name="message" class="form-control" required></textarea>
        </div>

        <button type="submit" name="submit" class="btn btn-primary">Submit</button>
    </form>
</section>

docs.title: Step 4: Create options.php (Admin Settings)
docs.text:
The options.php file is shown in Admin > Modules > [Your Module] > Settings.

<?php
// Save options
if (isset($_POST['mymodule_save'])) {
    $data = [
        'notify_email' => Typo::cleanX($_POST['notify_email']),
    ];
    Options::update('mymodule_options', json_encode($data));
}

$opt = json_decode(Options::get('mymodule_options'), true) ?? [];
?>
<section class="content">
    <h1><i class="fa fa-cogs"></i> My Module Settings</h1>
    <div class="box">
        <div class="box-body">
            <form method="POST">
                <div class="form-group">
                    <label>Notification Email</label>
                    <input type="email" name="notify_email" class="form-control"
                           value="<?= $opt['notify_email'] ?? '' ?>">
                </div>
                <button type="submit" name="mymodule_save" class="btn btn-primary">Save</button>
            </form>
        </div>
    </div>
</section>

docs.title: How Modules Are Loaded
docs.text:
When a user visits /mod/myPage.html (with Smart URL) or ?mod=myPage:

  1. Control::frontend() detects the mod route.
  2. Control::incFront('mod') includes Frontend/mod.control.php.
  3. mod.control.php fires Hooks::run('mod_control', $param).
  4. Your module's myPage($data) function executes, checking $data[0]['mod'].
/mod/myPage.html
  → Control::incFront('mod', $param)
  → mod.control.php
  → Hooks::run('mod_control', $param)
  → MyModule::myPage($data)
  → Mod::inc('myview', $page_data, '.../layout/')

docs.title: Useful Mod Class Methods
docs.text:
// Include a view file from a custom path
Mod::inc('view-name', $data, '/path/to/layout/');

// Register module page in menu manager
Mod::addMenuList(['hookName' => 'Display Label']);

docs.title: Available Hooks for Modules
docs.text: Hook Type Description mod_control Action Frontend page execution point admin_page_dashboard_action Action Add widget to admin dashboard admin_page_top_action Action Output at top of admin page admin_page_bottom_action Action Output at bottom of admin page footer_load_lib Action Add JS/CSS to page footer header_load_meta Action Add tags to HTML <head> post_content_filter Filter Modify post content before display post_submit_add_action Action Fires after a new post is saved user_login_action Action Fires after successful user login

docs.title: Complete Example: Contact Form Module
docs.text:
The built-in Contact Form module demonstrates this full pattern:

inc/mod/contact-form/
├── index.php               — Registers autoloader, instantiates Contact class
├── options.php             — Admin instructions panel
├── inc/
│   └── Contact.lib.php     — Main class: hooks into mod_control,
│                             handles POST, sends email via Mail::send()
└── layout/
    └── contactpage.php     — Latte form template

Key integration points from Contact.lib.php: 

// In constructor:
Hooks::attach('mod_control', ['Contact', 'contactPage']);
Mod::addMenuList(['contactPage' => 'Contact Us']);

// In contactPage():
if ($data[0]['mod'] == 'contactPage') {
    // Handle POST, validate, call Mail::send()
    Mod::inc('contactpage', $data, realpath(__DIR__.'/../layout/'));
}

docs.title: Installing the Module
docs.text:
1. Place your module folder in inc/mod/my-module/.
  2. Go to Admin > Modules.
  3. Find your module and click Install.
  4. To add a frontend page link, go to Admin > Menus and create a new menu item selecting Mod type with your module's page name.

docs.title: 1. Database Migrations
docs.text:
Don't use Db::query() in your constructor to create tables. Use the Migration System.

  1. Create a migration file in inc/migrations/.
  2. Use Db::query() within the migration's up() method to create your tables.
  3. Users will run php genix migrate to setup your module's database.

docs.title: 2. Access Control List (ACL)
docs.text:
Register custom permissions for your module features in your constructor:

Acl::register('MOD_MYMODULE_CAN_EXPORT', 'Allow users to export my module data');

Then check it before any sensitive action: 

if (Acl::check('MOD_MYMODULE_CAN_EXPORT')) {
    // Perform export...
}

docs.title: 3. API Resources
docs.text:
Expose your module data over the RESTful API:

  1. Create inc/lib/Control/Api/MyResourceApi.class.php.
  2. Your resource will be available at /api/v1/myresource/.

docs.title: Module Security (CSP & Assets)
docs.text: If your module injects scripts from external CDNs (like Google Maps or Chart.js), you must register these domains in the Content Security Policy whitelist.

docs.title: Registering Trusted Origins
docs.text:
Use the system_security_headers_args hook in your module's constructor:

// In inc/mod/my-module/inc/MyModule.lib.php
public function __construct() {
    Hooks::attach('system_security_headers_args', [$this, 'extendCSP']);
}

/**
 * Add trusted origins to the global CSP.
 */
public function extendCSP($rules) {
    if (!in_array("https://maps.googleapis.com", $rules['script-src'])) {
        $rules['script-src'][] = "https://maps.googleapis.com";
    }
    return $rules;
}

[!CAUTION] Avoid using http:// for external assets. The core security policy blocks insecure sources by default to prevent mixed content vulnerabilities.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks Class — All available hooks.
  • Mod Class — Module utilities.
  • Mail Class — Send emails from modules.
  • Options Class — Store and retrieve module settings.
  • Token Class — CSRF protection for module forms.
  • Control Layer Reference — How module routes are dispatched.
  • Model Class — Base ORM class.

docs.title: Theme Development Guide
docs.text: GeniXCMS features a powerful, decoupled theme engine that allows you to craft high-performance frontend experiences. This guide provides a step-by-step walkthrough for building a custom theme from the ground up, leveraging the Latte templating engine and the integrated Hooks system.

docs.title: ⚡ Quick Start: Using the CLI
docs.text: The fastest way to start a new theme is by using the built-in genix console. This command automatically generates the required directory and all boilerplate files (header.php, footer.php, index.php, etc.).

docs.title: Generate Scaffolding
docs.text:
In your project root, run:

php genix make:theme my-awesome-theme

docs.title: Activate the Theme
docs.text:
Once generated, you can instantly activate it via the CLI:

php genix theme:activate my-awesome-theme

docs.title: 🏗️ Theme Architecture
docs.text: Themes are located in inc/themes/{your-theme-name}/. A well-structured theme follows this standardized file layout:

docs.title: 📂 Core Required Files
docs.text: File Role themeinfo.php Metadata: Defines theme name, version, and developer info. header.php Head Section: Contains <head>, meta tags, and top navigation. footer.php Tail Section: Contains footer layout and closing tags. index.php Homepage: The default template for the root URL. single.php Post View: The template for individual blog posts.

docs.title: 📂 Logic & Customization
docs.text:
• function.php: Optional logic file to register hooks, load assets, and define theme classes.
  • options.php: Optional admin panel for theme-specific settings.

docs.title: 🚀 Step 1: Defining Theme Metadata (themeinfo.php)
docs.text:
GeniXCMS parses the comment block in this file to display theme information in the administration dashboard.

<?php
/*
 * Name: Modern Nexus
 * Desc: A clean, performance-optimized theme for GeniXCMS 2.0.
 * Version: 1.0.0
 * Developer: GeniXCMS Team
 * URI: https://genixcms.web.id
 * License: MIT License
 * Icon: bi bi-palette
 */

docs.title: 🎨 Step 2: The Shell (header.php & footer.php)
docs.text: GeniXCMS uses Latte, which means variables are accessible via {$variable} and logic via {if} or {foreach}.

docs.title: header.php
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="{$website_lang}">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>{$sitetitle|noescape} - {$site_name}</title>
    {$site_meta|noescape}
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="{$theme_url}/assets/css/style.css">
</head>
<body>
<nav class="navbar navbar-expand-lg">
    <div class="container">
        <a class="navbar-brand" href="{$site_url}">{$site_logo|noescape}</a>
        {Menus::getMenu('main')|noescape}
    </div>
</nav>

docs.title: footer.php
docs.text:
<footer class="bg-dark text-white py-4 mt-5">
    <div class="container text-center">
        {$site_footer|noescape}
    </div>
</footer>
{* Critical: Required to load system & module scripts *}
{Hooks::run('footer_load_lib')|noescape}
</body>
</html>

[!IMPORTANT] Don't Forget the Hooks: Always include {Hooks::run('footer_load_lib')|noescape} before </body>. Without it, core features like AJAX, popups, and module scripts will fail.

docs.title: 📝 Step 3: Content Templates (index.php)
docs.text:
The homepage template receives the $posts array automatically.

<main class="container py-5">
    <div class="row">
        {foreach $posts as $post}
        <div class="col-md-4 mb-4">
            <div class="card h-100 shadow-sm">
                <div class="card-body">
                    <h2 class="h5">
                        <a href="{Url::post($post->id)}">{$post->title|noescape}</a>
                    </h2>
                    <p class="text-muted small">
                         By {$post->author} | {Date::format($post->date)|noescape}
                    </p>
                    <p class="card-text">
                        {Posts::excerpt($post->content, 120)|noescape}
                    </p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>
        {/foreach}
    </div>
    <div class="d-flex justify-content-center mt-4">
        {$paging|noescape}
    </div>
</main>

docs.title: 🔧 Step 4: Theme Logic (function.php)
docs.text:
Use this file to register assets and hooks. It's best practice to wrap your logic in a Theme Class.

<?php
class NexusTheme {
    public function __construct() {
        // Register your assets
        Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', [$this, 'scripts']);
        Hooks::attach('header_load_meta', [$this, 'styles']);
    }

    public function styles() {
        echo '<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap-icons@1.11.0/font/bootstrap-icons.css">';
    }

    public function scripts() {
        echo '<script src="' . Url::theme() . '/assets/js/theme.js"></script>';
    }
}
new NexusTheme();

docs.title: 🛠️ Latte Syntax Quick Reference
docs.text: Action Syntax Print Variable {$var \| noescape} (Escaping is automatic by default) Include File {include 'sidebar.latte'} Looping {foreach $items as $item} ... {/foreach} Conditional {if $count > 0} ... {elseif $count == 0} ... {/if} PHP Method {ClassName::method($params) \| noescape}

docs.title: 🔒 Handling Security (CSP)
docs.text:
If your theme loads external scripts from a third-party CDN, you must whitelist the domain using the system_security_headers_args hook:

Hooks::attach('system_security_headers_args', function($rules) {
    // Whitelist a new CDN for scripts
    $rules['script-src'][] = "https://cdn.example.com";
    return $rules;
});

[!TIP] Pro Tip: Use the Vite Helper to integrate modern tools like Tailwind CSS and SCSS into your theme development workflow.

docs.title: Extending the Admin Navigation Menu
docs.text: GeniXCMS v2.0.0-alpha introduces a programmable admin menu system via the AdminMenu class. This allows modules and themes to add, remove, or reorder navigation items in the admin panel sidebar and top navbar without modifying any core file.

docs.title: 🏗️ Architecture Overview
docs.text:
The admin navigation is driven by a central registry. When the system boots, it:

  1. Loads all active Modules (new Mod())
  2. Loads the active Theme (new Theme())
  3. Fires the init hook
  4. Initializes AdminMenu (new AdminMenu()) — which registers core items and processes any items modules have already queued

This means you can safely call AdminMenu::add() from anywhere in your module or theme's function.php or index.php.

docs.title: 🔌 Registration in a Module
docs.text: The recommended location to register your admin menu items is your module's function.php file (loaded automatically on every page load when the module is active).

docs.title: Minimal Example
docs.text:
// inc/mod/my_shop/function.php

AdminMenu::add([
    'id'       => 'my_shop',
    'label'    => _('My Shop'),
    'icon'     => 'bi bi-cart3',
    'url'      => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=my_shop',
    'access'   => 3,
    'position' => 'external',
    'order'    => 10,
]);

docs.title: Full Example with Sub-Menu
docs.text:
// inc/mod/inventory/function.php

AdminMenu::add([
    'id'       => 'inventory',
    'label'    => _('Inventory'),
    'icon'     => 'bi bi-box-seam-fill',
    'url'      => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=inventory',
    'access'   => 2,           // Editor and above
    'position' => 'external',
    'order'    => 20,
    'children' => [
        [
            'label'  => _('Products'),
            'icon'   => 'bi bi-boxes',
            'url'    => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=inventory&tab=products',
            'access' => 2,
        ],
        [
            'label'  => _('Orders'),
            'icon'   => 'bi bi-receipt',
            'url'    => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=inventory&tab=orders',
            'access' => 2,
        ],
        [
            'label'  => _('Stock Report'),
            'icon'   => 'bi bi-bar-chart-line',
            'url'    => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=inventory&tab=report',
            'access' => 1,    // Supervisor only for reports
        ],
    ],
]);

docs.title: 🎨 Registration in a Theme
docs.text:
If your theme provides its own admin utilities (e.g., a Theme Options shortcut), register the item in your theme's function.php.

// inc/themes/my_theme/function.php

AdminMenu::add([
    'id'       => 'my_theme_options',
    'label'    => _('Theme Options'),
    'icon'     => 'bi bi-brush-fill',
    'url'      => 'index.php?page=themes&act=options',
    'access'   => 0,          // Administrator only
    'position' => 'external',
    'order'    => 5,
]);

docs.title: 📐 Menu Positions
docs.text:
Choose the right position to place your item in the correct region:

Position Sidebar Section Top Navbar Behavior main Under "Main Navigation" Shown as top-level nav links management Under "Management" Shown as top-level or dropdown settings Under Settings group Shown inside "Settings" dropdown external Under "External" Shown inside "External" dropdown

[!TIP] Best Practice: Use external for module-specific items. Reserve main and management for items that have broad, primary importance across the whole site.

docs.title: 🌲 Adding Sub-Menus to Existing Items
docs.text: If you want to inject a sub-menu into an existing parent item (e.g., adding a "Draft Store" link under the "Posts" menu or a "Custom Settings" under "Settings"), use AdminMenu::addChild().

docs.title: Adding a Single Sub-Menu
docs.text:
// Add a custom link under the core "Posts" menu
AdminMenu::addChild('posts', [
    'label'  => _('My Post Type'),
    'url'    => 'index.php?page=posts&type=custom',
    'icon'   => 'bi bi-star',
    'access' => 4,   // Author level and above
]);

docs.title: Adding Multiple Sub-Menus at Once
docs.text:
Use AdminMenu::addChildren() to inject a batch of items into a parent.

AdminMenu::addChildren('settings', [
    [
        'label' => _('Store Settings'),
        'url'   => 'index.php?page=settings&tab=store',
    ],
    [
        'label' => _('API Configuration'),
        'url'   => 'index.php?page=settings&tab=api',
    ]
]);

[!TIP] Parent IDs: Common core parent IDs include posts, pages, users, settings, themes, modules, permissions, comments, and media.

docs.title: 🔄 Removing Core Menu Items
docs.text:
You can remove any core menu item using its id. This is useful for building specialized or restricted admin panels.

// Remove core items your custom installation does not need
AdminMenu::remove('comments');    // Remove Comments from sidebar
AdminMenu::remove('permissions'); // Remove ACL Manager link

[!CAUTION] Removing core items may confuse users who expect standard navigation. Only do this if your module or client installation explicitly requires a simplified interface.

docs.title: 🔢 Controlling Order
docs.text:
All core items use order values that are multiples of 10. Use values between them to inject items precisely:

order: 10  → Posts
order: 15  → 📌 My Custom Item  ← injected between Posts and Pages
order: 20  → Pages
order: 30  → Comments

AdminMenu::add([
    'id'    => 'newsletter',
    'label' => _('Newsletter'),
    'icon'  => 'bi bi-envelope-paper',
    'url'   => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=newsletter',
    'position' => 'main',
    'order' => 15,   // Appears between Posts (10) and Pages (20)
]);

docs.title: 🧩 Using Access Control
docs.text:
The access key controls which user levels can see the menu item. It uses the same numeric role system as User::access():

// Only administrators see this item
AdminMenu::add([
    ...
    'access' => 0,
]);

// Authors and above
AdminMenu::add([
    ...
    'access' => 3,
]);

Child items can also have their own access level, which overrides the parent:

'children' => [
    ['label' => 'Public Stats',  'url' => '...', 'access' => 4], // Authors+
    ['label' => 'Private Stats', 'url' => '...', 'access' => 0], // Admin only
],

docs.title: 🔌 Using the init Hook (Advanced)
docs.text:
For items that depend on runtime conditions (e.g., feature flags or options), you can defer registration to the init hook:

// inc/mod/my_module/function.php

Hooks::attach('init', function() {
    if (Options::v('my_module_premium') === 'on') {
        AdminMenu::add([
            'id'       => 'my_module_premium',
            'label'    => _('Premium Features'),
            'icon'     => 'bi bi-star-fill',
            'url'      => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=my_module&tab=premium',
            'position' => 'external',
            'order'    => 30,
        ]);
    }
});

[!IMPORTANT] The init hook fires before new AdminMenu(), so items registered via init are available when the core menu is being built.

docs.title: 📋 Complete Reference
docs.text: Method Description AdminMenu::add(array $item) Register a new menu item. AdminMenu::remove(string $id) Unregister a menu item by ID. AdminMenu::getItems(?string $pos) Get sorted array of items, optionally filtered by position. AdminMenu::renderSidebar(?string $pos) Output sidebar <li> HTML for given position. AdminMenu::renderTopNav(?string $pos) Output top navbar <li> HTML for given position.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• AdminMenu Class Reference — Full API documentation.
  • Create a Module — How to build a complete GeniXCMS module.
  • Create a Theme — How to build a custom admin-aware theme.
  • Using Hooks — Deferred registration using the init hook.

docs.title: How to Use the Internal Ajax API
docs.text:
GeniXCMS provides an internal JSON-based API accessible at /ajax/api/. This is primarily intended for internal frontend interactions (like search suggestions or theme widgets) and requires a valid CSRF session token.

[!IMPORTANT] For external applications, mobile apps, or headless CMS integrations, please use the RESTful API (v1) which uses secure API Keys and standard REST terminology.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text:
{site_url}/ajax/api/{TOKEN}?action={action}

For non-Smart URL mode: 

{site_url}/index.php?ajax=api&token={TOKEN}&action={action}

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: The API uses CSRF tokens for protected endpoints. Public endpoints do not require a token.

docs.title: Getting a Token
docs.text:
The current session token is available as the TOKEN constant in templates:

{* In a Latte template *}
<script>
    const API_TOKEN = '{TOKEN}';
</script>

In PHP: 

$token = TOKEN; // Available globally after session is initialized

docs.title: recent_posts (Public)
docs.text:
Returns the most recent posts with metadata and thumbnail URLs.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=recent_posts&num=5&type=post

Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description num int 5 Number of posts to return type string 'post' Content type: 'post' or 'page' cat int (none) Filter by category ID

Response: 

{
    "status": "success",
    "data": [
        {
            "id": 42,
            "title": "My Post Title",
            "url": "https://example.com/my-post.html",
            "date": "26 March 2026",
            "author": "admin",
            "author_url": "https://example.com/author/admin/",
            "category": "Technology",
            "category_url": "https://example.com/category/1/technology/",
            "image": "https://example.com/thumb/type/square/size/300/...",
            "excerpt": "A short preview of the post content..."
        }
    ]
}

docs.title: categories (Public)
docs.text:
Returns all categories of a given type.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=categories&type=post

Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description type string 'post' Taxonomy type: 'post' or 'page'

Response: 

{
    "status": "success",
    "data": [
        { "id": 1, "name": "Technology", "slug": "technology" },
        { "id": 2, "name": "Travel", "slug": "travel" }
    ]
}

docs.title: authors (Public)
docs.text:
Returns a unique list of usernames who have published content.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=authors

Response: 

{
    "status": "success",
    "data": [
        { "author": "admin" },
        { "author": "p_wijayanto" }
    ]
}

docs.title: tags (Token Required)
docs.text:
Returns tag suggestions matching a search term. Used for autocomplete.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=tags&term=php

Parameters:

Parameter Type Description term string Prefix to search for matching tag names

docs.title: JavaScript Usage Example
docs.text:
const SITE_URL = 'https://example.com';
const TOKEN = '...'; // from your Latte template

// Fetch recent posts
fetch(`${SITE_URL}/ajax/api/${TOKEN}?action=recent_posts&num=6`)
    .then(res => res.json())
    .then(({ status, data }) => {
        if (status !== 'success') return;
        data.forEach(post => {
            console.log(post.title, post.url);
        });
    });

docs.title: Error Responses
docs.text: Scenario Response Invalid or missing token (protected action) {"status":"error","message":"Token not exist or invalid"} Unknown action {"status":"error","message":"Unknown action"}

docs.title: Adding Custom API Actions
docs.text:
You can extend the API from a module by listening to the api-ajax controller. Create a custom Ajax controller at:

inc/mod/my-module/ajax/myaction-ajax.control.php

Or attach to an existing hook if available:

// In your module class constructor:
Hooks::attach('api_action_myaction', ['MyModule', 'handleApiAction']);

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Control Layer Reference — How AJAX routes are dispatched.
  • Token Class — How CSRF tokens are generated and validated.
  • Http Class — For making outbound HTTP requests from the server side.

docs.title: Using Virtual Cron
docs.text:
GeniXCMS v2.0.0-alpha introduces the Cron class, allowing modules and themes to schedule recurring or one-time tasks without needing a system-level crontab.

GeniXCMS's cron is "virtual"—it checks for due tasks on every visitor's request.

docs.title: 🚀 Basic Usage
docs.text:
To use the Cron system, you typically:

  1. Attach a function to a custom hook.
  2. Schedule that hook using the Cron class.

docs.title: 🐘 1. Attach a Hook
docs.text:
Your module or theme should define what happens when the cron runs:

// in inc/mod/my_module/function.php
Hooks::attach('my_daily_sync', function($args) {
    // Perform sync logic here...
    // $args will be an array of arguments passed during scheduling
});

docs.title: 🗓️ 2. Schedule the Task
docs.text:
It is best to schedule the task once when the system starts up or during module activation:

// Schedule a daily task
if (!Cron::getNext('my_daily_sync')) { // Check if already scheduled
    Cron::schedule(time(), 'daily', 'my_daily_sync', ['force' => true]);
}

docs.title: 🔍 Checking Scheduled Tasks
docs.text:
You can check when the next run for a specific hook is scheduled:

$next_run = Cron::getNext('my_daily_sync');
if ($next_run) {
    echo "Next run: " . date('Y-m-d H:i:s', $next_run);
}

docs.title: 🗑️ Clearing Schedules
docs.text:
If you no longer need a scheduled task (e.g., when a module is deactivated), you should clear it:

Cron::clear('my_daily_sync');

docs.title: 🔧 One-Time Events
docs.text:
To run a task exactly once in the future:

// Run 30 minutes from now
Cron::scheduleOnce(time() + 1800, 'send_follow_up_email', ['user_id' => 45]);

docs.title: ➕ Adding Custom Intervals
docs.text:
By default, hourly, twicedaily, daily, and weekly are available. You can add more using the cron_schedules filter:

Hooks::attach('cron_schedules', function($schedules) {
    $schedules['every_five_mins'] = [
        'interval' => 300,
        'display'  => _('Every 5 Minutes')
    ];
    return $schedules;
});

docs.title: ⚙️ Triggering via Real System Cron (Better Performance)
docs.text:
While virtual cron works on every request, it can cause slight delays for visitors or might not run at all if there is no traffic.

For high-traffic or mission-critical sites, you can trigger the cron via a real crontab on your server:

# Add to your system crontab (e.g. crontab -e)
* * * * * curl https://your-site.com/index.php > /dev/null 2>&1

Or you can create a dedicated PHP script that only runs Cron::run().

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Cron Class Reference — Full API documentation.
  • Using Hooks — How hooks work in GeniXCMS.
  • Create a Module — Best practices for module initialization.

docs.title: How to Use Hooks
docs.text:
Hooks are the primary extension mechanism in GeniXCMS. They allow themes and modules to add functionality or modify output without editing core files.

There are two types of hooks:

  • Actions — Execute a function at a specific point (no return value expected).
  • Filters — Modify a value and return the modified result.

docs.title: Attaching an Action
docs.text:
Usage: Hooks::attach(string $hook_name, callable $callback, int $priority = 10);

// Attach a function to the 'footer_load_lib' hook
Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['MyTheme', 'loadAssets']);

// Or with a closure
Hooks::attach('post_submit_add_action', function($data) {
    // Send a notification email when a post is added
    Mail::send([
        'to'      => 'admin@example.com',
        'subject' => 'New Post Published',
        'message' => 'A new post was just published.',
    ]);
});

docs.title: Running an Action
docs.text:
Usage: Hooks::run(string $hook_name, mixed $data = '');

// In a theme template (Latte)
{Hooks::run('footer_load_lib')|noescape}

// In PHP code
Hooks::run('post_submit_add_action', $_POST);

docs.title: Attaching a Filter
docs.text:
Usage: Hooks::filter(string $hook_name, mixed $value);

The attached function must accept the value and return a (modified) value.

// Attach a filter to modify post titles
Hooks::attach('post_title_filter', function($title) {
    return strtoupper($title); // uppercase all titles
});

docs.title: Applying a Filter
docs.text:
// In PHP
$title = Hooks::filter('post_title_filter', $post->title);

// In a Latte template
{Hooks::filter('post_title_filter', $post->title)|noescape}

docs.title: Action Hooks
docs.text: Hook Trigger Point $data Type footer_load_lib Just before </body> in every page (none) header_load_meta Inside <head> of every page (none) init Standard system startup finished (none) post_submit_add_action After a new post is saved $_POST array post_sqldel_action After a post is deleted from database int $id admin_sidebar_start Before the first admin sidebar menu (none) admin_sidebar_end After the last admin sidebar menu (none) theme_activated_action After a theme is successfully activated string $theme_name admin_page_dashboard_action On the admin dashboard $data array admin_footer_action At the bottom of every admin page (none) user_login_action After a successful user login $data array user_register_action After a new user registers $data array

docs.title: Filter Hooks
docs.text: Hook What It Filters post_title_filter Post title string before display post_pre_insert_filter Modify entire post data before inserting to DB post_pre_update_filter Modify entire post data before updating in DB user_pre_insert_filter Modify entire user data before creating user user_pre_update_filter Modify entire user data before updating post_submit_title_filter Title string during save (sanitize) post_submit_content_filter Content string during save (sanitize) post_content_filter Post body HTML before render system_security_headers_args CSP rules array (directives and sources)

docs.title: Priority
docs.text:
Attach multiple callbacks to the same hook. Lower priority numbers run first.

Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['Analytics', 'load'], 5);  // runs first
Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['Ads', 'load'], 20);        // runs later

docs.title: Registering Hooks in a Theme
docs.text:
Put all hook registrations inside the theme class constructor in function.php:

class MyTheme
{
    public function __construct()
    {
        Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['MyTheme', 'loadJs']);
        Hooks::attach('header_load_meta', ['MyTheme', 'loadCss']);
        Hooks::attach('post_title_filter', ['MyTheme', 'addIcon']);
    }

    public static function loadJs()
    {
        echo '<script src="' . Url::theme() . '/js/app.js"></script>';
    }

    public static function loadCss()
    {
        echo '<link rel="stylesheet" href="' . Url::theme() . '/css/style.css">';
    }

    public static function addIcon($title)
    {
        return '📖 ' . $title;
    }
}
new MyTheme();

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks Class API — Full method reference.
  • Create a Theme — Where to register hooks in a theme.
  • Create a Module — Using hooks in modules.

docs.title: Using UiBuilder & OptionsBuilder
docs.text: This guide provides a comprehensive overview of the two primary layout engines in GeniXCMS v2.0+: UiBuilder and OptionsBuilder. These tools are designed to standardize the administrative interface into a modern, compact, "industrial ERP" aesthetic.

docs.title: 1. OptionsBuilder
docs.text: OptionsBuilder is a specialized engine for creating professional theme and module options panels. It features a fixed three-section layout: Topbar (for saving), Sidebar (for tab navigation), and Main Content (for settings).

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Fluent Interface: Designed for rapid configuration of complex settings.
  • Built-in Typography Group: Handles font-family, size, weight, and color in a single row.
  • Color Presets: Support for quick style presets that users can apply with one click.
  • Frontend CSS Generator: Automatically generates responsive CSS and Google Font imports based on saved settings.

docs.title: Schema Definition
docs.text:
An OptionsBuilder schema is an array of "tabs". Each tab contains "cards" or "sections".

$schema = [
    [
        'id'    => 'general',
        'label' => 'General Settings',
        'icon'  => 'bi bi-gear',
        'group' => 'Core', // Sidebar grouping
        'cards' => [
            [
                'title' => 'Branding',
                'cols'  => 2, // Grid columns
                'fields' => [
                    ['type' => 'text', 'name' => 'site_name', 'label' => 'Site Name'],
                    ['type' => 'color', 'name' => 'primary_color', 'label' => 'Primary Accent']
                ]
            ]
        ]
    ]
];

docs.title: Implementation Pattern
docs.text:
$opt = Options::get_all(); // Get all settings
$builder = new OptionsBuilder($opt);
$builder->render($schema);

docs.title: 2. UiBuilder
docs.text: UiBuilder is a generic, highly flexible engine for creating module pages, dashboards, and CRUD forms. Unlike OptionsBuilder, it is non-opinionated about the overall page structure and can be integrated into any gxadmin view.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Dynamic Header: Supports sticky headers with breadcrumbs and action buttons.
  • Nested Grid System: Build complex layouts using rows and columns.
  • Diverse Elements: Support for stats cards, alerts, switch toggles, raw HTML, and data tables.
  • Modern Aesthetic: Hardcoded to use Bootstrap 5 + GeniXCMS ERP styles.

docs.title: Schema Structure
docs.text:
UiBuilder uses a hierarchical schema:

  1. Header: Page identity and global actions.
  2. Tabs (Optional): Navigation within the page.
  3. Content: A list of row or card elements.

docs.title: Example: Media Settings Schema
docs.text:
$schema = [
    'header' => [
        'title' => 'Media Library',
        'button' => [
            'type' => 'button', 'name' => 'change', 'label' => 'Save', 
            'icon' => 'bi bi-save', 'class' => 'btn btn-primary'
        ]
    ],
    'content' => [
        ['type' => 'row', 'items' => [
            ['width' => 8, 'content' => [
                ['type' => 'card', 'title' => 'Configuration', 'body_elements' => [
                    ['type' => 'input', 'name' => 'path', 'label' => 'Storage Path']
                ]]
            ]],
            ['width' => 4, 'content' => [
                ['type' => 'alert', 'style' => 'info', 'content' => 'Tip: Use WebP for speed.']
            ]]
        ]]
    ]
];

docs.title: Field Types Comparison
docs.text: Field Type OptionsBuilder UiBuilder Description text / input ✅ ✅ Standard text input. number ✅ ✅ Numeric input with min/max. select ✅ ✅ Dropdown menu. toggle / switch ✅ ✅ Boolean on/off switch. color ✅ ✅ Hex color picker + text input. range ✅ ❌ Slider with unit badge. typo_row ✅ ❌ Multi-field typography row. stats_card ❌ ✅ Dashboard-style metric display. tab_mode sidebar js / link Navigation logic.

docs.title: 1. Centralized Notifications
docs.text:
Always include the notification hook before rendering the form: 

echo Hooks::run('admin_page_notif_action', $data);

docs.title: 2. Form Submission
docs.text:
Wrap UiBuilder in a form and include the security token: 

echo '<form method="POST">';
$builder = new UiBuilder($schema);
$builder->render();
echo '<input type="hidden" name="token" value="'.TOKEN.'">';
echo '</form>';

docs.title: 3. Sticky Headers
docs.text: UiBuilder headers are sticky by default. Ensure enough bottom padding in your content to prevent the footer from overlapping or content from being hidden behind the header on scroll.

docs.title: 4. Naming Convention
docs.text:
• Use Snake Case for OptionsBuilder field names (e.g., theme_primary_color).
  • Use Explicit Names for UiBuilder buttons to avoid collisions (e.g., 'name' => 'save_mod_settings').

docs.title: 1. Typography Prefix Mapping
docs.text:
When using typo_row in OptionsBuilder, ensure you use these standard prefixes to leverage the automatic CSS generation engine:

  • typo_body: Controls main document flow and paragraphs.
  • typo_nav: Targets the navigation bar and primary link structures.
  • typo_brand: Specifically for site-wide logo/identity typography.
  • typo_h1 - typo_h6: Granular control over the page's heading hierarchy.
  • typo_meta: For article data like dates, author names, and taxonomies.
  • typo_list: Standardizes unordered and ordered list presentations.
  • typo_blockquote: Controls stylistic citations and blockquote elements.
  • typo_pagination: For UI navigation buttons in multi-page indexes.

docs.title: 2. Overriding Framework Styles
docs.text:
You can pass theme or module-specific overrides using the extraCssRules parameter in the OptionsBuilder::generateFrontendCSS configuration array: 

{OptionsBuilder::generateFrontendCSS($opt, [
    'extraCssRules' => '.my-special-selector { color: var(--primary-color) !important; }'
])|noescape}

docs.title: 3. Global Notification & Toast System
docs.text:
GeniXCMS v2.0+ utilizes a unified AJAX notification system provided by OptionsBuilder:

  • ID Requirement: Ensure your success/error elements use #gx-toast-container for the wrapper and .gx-toast for individual messages.
  • Auto-Extraction: The framework's AJAX handler automatically detects and displays these notifications from any theme's options.php response.

docs.title: 4. Instance vs Static Calls
docs.text:
• Use new OptionsBuilder($opt) for rendering the admin panel.
  • Use OptionsBuilder::renderCSS() (static) to inject admin-side styles.
  • Use OptionsBuilder::generateFrontendCSS() (static) in your theme's frontend header.

docs.title: Vite for Theme Asset Bundling
docs.text: GeniXCMS 2.0.0-alpha introduces native, first-class support for Vite. This integration transforms your frontend development workflow, allowing you to leverage modern tools like SCSS, Tailwind CSS, TypeScript, and ES Modules with near-instant Hot Module Replacement (HMR).

docs.title: 🏗️ Prerequisites
docs.text:
Ensure your local development environment is equipped with:

  • ✅ Node.js (v18.0.0 or higher)
  • ✅ Package Manager (NPM, PNPM, or Yarn)
  • ✅ GeniXCMS v2.0.0-alpha installed and running.

docs.title: 📂 Project Structure
docs.text:
To utilize the Vite engine, your theme should follow this standardized asset organization:

inc/themes/my-modern-theme/
├── assets/
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── main.js         ← Entry Point (JS/TS)
│   │   └── style.scss      ← Main Stylesheet
│   └── dist/               ← Build output (auto-generated)
├── index.php
├── package.json            ← Dependencies & Scripts
└── vite.config.js          ← Bundler Configuration

docs.title: ⚙️ Vite Configuration
docs.text:
The vite.config.js file is the brain of your asset pipeline. Use the following baseline configuration to ensure seamless integration with the CMS:

import { defineConfig } from 'vite';

export default defineConfig({
  build: {
    manifest: true,
    outDir: 'assets/dist',
    emptyOutDir: true,
    rollupOptions: {
      input: 'assets/src/main.js',
    },
  },
  server: {
    cors: true,
    strictPort: true,
    port: 5173,
    hmr: {
        host: 'localhost',
    }
  },
});

docs.title: 🔌 Theme Integration
docs.text:
GeniXCMS provides a dedicated Vite helper class to manage asset injection. In your theme's header.php, simply add:

<!-- Inject Vite HMR client or Manifest-based production bundle -->
<?= Vite::client('assets/src/main.js'); ?>

[!IMPORTANT] Dynamic Loading: The Vite::client() helper is intelligent. It detects if Dev Mode (HMR) is enabled in your site settings. If enabled, it connects to the Vite dev server; otherwise, it resolves hashed filenames from manifest.json.

docs.title: 🚀 Development Workflow
docs.text:
Follow these steps to experience modern theme development:

  1. Dependencies: Install the necessary Node packages. 
    npm install
  2. Toggle HMR: Go to Settings > General and ensure Hot Reloading is enabled.
  3. Start Dev Server: Launch the Vite server in your theme directory. 
    npm run dev
  4. Develop: Edit your SCSS or JS files. Your changes will refresh instantly in the browser without a full page reload.

docs.title: 📦 Production Deployment
docs.text:
When your theme is ready for the world, compile it for maximum performance:

  1. Build Assets: Generate optimized, minified files. 
    npm run build
  2. Disable HMR: In Settings > General, turn off Hot Reloading.
  3. Deploy: GeniXCMS will now automatically serve versioned, cache-basted assets from assets/dist/.

[!TIP] Tailwind CSS Integration: To use Tailwind, simply run npx tailwindcss init -p, configure your template paths in tailwind.config.js, and @import Tailwind into your style.scss. Vite's PostCSS plugin will handle all the heavy lifting!

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Vite Helper API — Detailed reference for the Vite PHP class.
  • Theme Development Guide — How to build a full theme around your assets.

docs.title: Access Control List (ACL) Management
docs.text: GeniXCMS 2.0.0-alpha features a robust, fine-grained Access Control List (ACL) that allows you to define exactly what each user group can see and do within the administration dashboard.

docs.title: 🧭 Group-Based Permissions
docs.text:
The ACL system is built around the 4 core system groups, though individual access levels can be specified for more granular control.

System Group Access Level Description Administrator 0 Has full access to every core and module feature. Moderator 1-2 Typically manages content and user interactions. Editor 3-4 Focused solely on content creation and modification. General Member 5+ Basic frontend interaction and profile management.

docs.title: 🏗️ Modifying Permissions (ACL Matrix)
docs.text:
To fine-tune permissions for your site's human resources:

  1. Navigate to Admin > Users > ACL Manager.
  2. The ACL Matrix will display a grid of all registered permission hooks (e.g., POST_ADD, THEME_ACTIVATE, SETTINGS_EDIT).
  3. Configuring: Use the dropdowns to set the Minimum Access Level required for each permission.
  4. Save: Click Update Permissions to apply the new security rules across the application.

docs.title: 🏹 Built-in Security Hooks
docs.text:
GeniXCMS registers several dozen security hooks by default.

  • Post Management: POST_ADD, POST_EDIT, POST_DELETE.
  • System Admin: SETTINGS_EDIT, THEME_ACTIVATE, MODULE_INSTALL.
  • User Governance: USER_ADD, USER_EDIT, ACL_EDIT.

docs.title: 🔌 Modules & Custom ACLs
docs.text:
Third-party modules can safely register their own custom permissions to the matrix.

  • Developer Hook: To register a new permission, use Acl::register('MOD_MYPERMISSION', 'Description'); in your module's constructor.
  • Check Access: Always call if (Acl::check('MOD_MYPERMISSION')) before executing any sensitive logic in your code.

[!CAUTION] Privilege Escalation: Be extremely careful when lowering the requirements for ACL_EDIT. Only Level 0 (Administrator) accounts should typically have the capability to modify the ACL matrix.

[!TIP] Audit Tip: Regularly audit your ACL matrix to ensure that no lower-level group has gained access to sensitive system commands during a new module installation.

docs.title: Output Caching & Performance Settings
docs.text: GeniXCMS's built-in Cache Engine significantly reduces your site's load times and server resource consumption by storing previously rendered pages as static files.

docs.title: 🎨 Managing Output Delivery
docs.text:
To configure your site's caching behavior:

  • Navigate to Settings > General in the administrative sidebar.
  • Look for the Caching and Performance configuration blocks.

docs.title: 🏗️ Activating Static Caching
docs.text:
Static file-based caching is a first-class feature that avoids complex database lookups for repeat visitors.

  1. Toggle: Set Enable Caching to "Yes" to start archiving your site's static assets and pages.
  2. Lifetime: Configure how long each cached version remain valid (default: 3600 seconds).
  3. HMR Support: In Dev Mode, caching is automatically bypassed to ensure developers see live changes immediately.

docs.title: 📐 Caching Architecture
docs.text: Resource Type Cache Strategy Static Pages Indefinite (Updated only on manual invalidation). Blog Posts Timed (Automatically invalidated on new comment or edit). Category Archives Timed (Invalidated on content updates).

docs.title: 🛠️ Security & Management
docs.text:
• Storage Path: All cached files are archived in the assets/cache/ directory.
  • Manual Invalidation: You can clear the entire system cache through the Settings Dashboard whenever you perform a major update.
  • Access Control: Admins can specify which user levels (e.g., Level 0) have permission to clear the cache.

[!CAUTION] Disk Space: Large sites with thousands of pages can consume significant storage in the assets/cache/ directory. Monitor your site's storage capacity regularly.

[!TIP] Performance Hint: Combined with Vite asset bundling and a high-performance PHP 8.⅜.4 engine, GeniXCMS's caching results in sub-second page loads.

docs.title: Organizing Content with Categories
docs.text: Categories provide the primary structural hierarchy for your site's content. They allow you to group related posts together, improving your site’s navigation, user experience, and search engine discoverability.

docs.title: 🧭 Managing Your Taxonomy
docs.text: To access the category management interface: - Navigate to the Categories menu in the left administrative sidebar. - The Category Manager dashboard will display your current organizational tree.

docs.title: 🏗️ Creating a New Category
docs.text:
It is recommended to define your core categories before you begin publishing posts.

  1. Add New: Click the Add Category button in the header section.
  2. Modal Settings: A configuration window will appear.
  3. Parenting: Choose a Parent Category if you are creating a sub-category. Leave as "None" for primary, top-level categories.
  4. Identification: Enter a clear, descriptive Category Name.
  5. Save: Click Save Changes to commit your new category to the database.

docs.title: 🌳 Understanding Category Hierarchy
docs.text:
Categories in GeniXCMS support a nested, tree-like structure:

  • Main Category: Primary topics that define your site's main sections.
  • Sub-Category: Specialized niches within a main category (e.g., "Web Dev" under "Programming").

docs.title: 🛠️ Modifying & Refining
docs.text:
• Naming: Ensure your category names are concise.
  • Organization: You can re-parent categories at any time to reorganize your site's structure as it grows.
  • Deletion: When deleting a category, ensure you reassess the posts currently assigned to it.

[!TIP] SEO Best Practice: Use categories for broad grouping (e.g., News, Tutorials) and use Tags for specific details (e.g., PHP, Laravel, MkDocs). This creates a logical, searchable map for both users and search engines.

docs.title: GeniXCMS CLI Console (genix) Documentation
docs.text: genix is the built-in Command-Line Interface (CLI) for GeniXCMS, designed to help administrators and developers manage the system without accessing the backend dashboard. Tasks such as database management, module and theme scaffolding, user creation, and cache clearing can be executed directly through this console.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
This CLI script is located in the root directory of your GeniXCMS installation. You can run it via the terminal using your PHP binary.

Basic Command: 

php genix <command> [arguments]

To view the full list of available commands directly from your terminal, you can type: 

php genix help

docs.title: 1. ⚙️ System Utility
docs.text:
A collection of commands to view and manage core features and utilities of the application.

Command Arguments Description serve [port] Starts the built-in PHP development server at http://localhost:8000 (or a port based on your provided argument). The site will instantly load similar to using XAMPP/Laragon. Use Ctrl-C to stop the server. sys:info - Displays environment diagnostics such as the PHP version, server OS, Server Time, and detailed Database Client identifier. Very useful for initial troubleshooting. options:list - Displays all System Options in your database table along with their values (partially truncated if too long). Good for peeking at hidden configurations. options:set <key> <value> Forcibly changes an option or adds a new one to GeniXCMS. Example: php genix options:set sitename "New Website" cache:clear - Clears data cache stack as well as Latte template cache files in the assets/cache folder. Frequently used after modifying a template if changes are not immediately visible.

docs.title: 2. 🏗️ Developer Scaffolding Commands
docs.text:
Special commands for developers to generate boilerplates (raw code structure), eliminating the need to manually create directories and files.

Command Required Arguments Description make:theme <name> Generates a new Latte Engine-based theme file structure in /inc/themes/. This command automatically creates the assets folder and template files (header.php, footer.php, index.php, single.php, page.php, and themeinfo.php). Example: php genix make:theme e-commerce make:module <name> Generates scaffolding for a new custom module (plugin) inside the /inc/mod/ directory. PHP classes and docblock comments/metadata are immediately validated and automatically created. Example: php genix make:module payment

docs.title: 3. 👥 User Management
docs.text:
Functions similarly to account management via the dashboard panel, but manually through the command-line.

Command Required Arguments / Options Description user:create <u> <e> <p> [g] Registers a new user by strictly providing arguments in order (username, email, raw password). Includes an optional argument for the Access Group level (0 = Administrator, 4 = Specific Contributor, etc). Example: php genix user:create admin admin@web.com passMe123 0 user:list - Displays all registered user entities complete with their ID, Username, Email, and Action Group Name details natively in a console table. user:delete <id> Permanently removes a user from the database (gx_user and gx_user_detail) based on their numerical unique ID. Example: php genix user:delete 3

docs.title: 4. 🎨 Theme & Module
docs.text:
Terminal-based functions to forcibly toggle Extensions registered in the site repository. Helpful if your site encounters a white screen due to a theme error and you must disable it from the backend server.

Command Required Arguments Description theme:list - Polls the database to list all available system themes, their active status, and release versions out of their respective metadata configurations. theme:activate <folder_name> Switches your Active Theme to the targeted slug folder. Example: php genix theme:activate gneex module:list - Lists all installed modules completely with their respective activity status. module:activate <folder_name> Activates a specific module. Example: php genix module:activate seonix module:deactivate <folder_name> Deactivates a module from its running execution. Example: php genix module:deactivate seonix

docs.title: 5. 🗄️ Database Migrations
docs.text:
This feature tracks the chronological migration history of database schemas and data (utilizing GeniXCMS's installation and upgrade process).

Command Required Arguments Description migrate - Recursively executes and records all unexecuted historical files located within inc/migrations/. migrate:rollback - Unrolls/Rolls back the last executed batch of completed migration files. migrate:create FileName Generates a table installation module boiler script with automatic Timestamping and a ready-to-fill PHP class in inc/migrations/. Example: php genix migrate:create UpdateUserTable

docs.title: Comment Settings & Interactivity
docs.text: GeniXCMS's built-in Comment Engine provides a robust way for your audience to engage with your content. It features a full moderation suite to prevent spam and ensure the quality of user interactions.

docs.title: 🎨 Managing Audience Engagement
docs.text: To configure your site's comment behavior: - Navigate to Settings > General in the administrative sidebar. - Look for the Comments and Moderation configuration blocks.

docs.title: 🏗️ Activating Reader Feedback
docs.text:
Feedback is a powerful tool for community building and SEO.

  1. Toggle: Set Enable Comments to "Yes" to activate the comment form on all post types.
  2. Moderation: Choose whether comments are published instantly or require approval from a Level 0 (Administrator).
  3. HMR Support: Using modern themes with Vite HMR allows for instant visual updates of comment sections during development.

docs.title: 📐 Moderation Workflow
docs.text:
GeniXCMS features a specialized management dashboard for handling feedback.

Status Capability Pending Content that is held back for review before being publicly visible. Approved Public comments that are now live on your posts. Marked as Spam Content that is flagged and removed from the public view.

docs.title: 🛠️ Security & Integrity
docs.text:
• CAPTCHA Support: GeniXCMS's Xaptcha class provides a simple, anti-spam challenge to ensure that commenters are human.
  • XSS Protection: All user-generated content is sanitized through HTMLPurifier to prevent malicious code execution.
  • Access Control: You can allow or disallow commenting capabilities based on user registration status.

[!CAUTION] Anti-Spam Strategy: It is highly recommended to enable Mandatory Moderation for first-time commenters to protect the reputation and integrity of your site.

[!TIP] Developer Hint: Customizing your comment layout? Use the {Hooks::run('post_comment_display')|noescape} hook in your theme to inject a custom feedback interface.

docs.title: System Configuration
docs.text: GeniXCMS provides a centralized management interface to fine-tune your website's behavior, SEO metadata, and technical integrations. Access these tools by navigating to Settings in the main administration sidebar.

docs.title: 🌐 General Settings
docs.text:
This section defines the core identity of your website.

Field Purpose Site Name The primary title of your website. Site Slogan A brief, catchy phrase that appears next to your site name. Keywords Comma-separated terms for search engine indexing. Description A concise summary of your site's content for SEO snippets. Site E-Mail The default address used for system notifications and "From" headers.

docs.title: 📧 E-Mail & SMTP
docs.text: Configure how your server dispatches outgoing communications.

docs.title: 📤 PHP Mail (Default)
docs.text: Recommended for simple hosting environments. It uses the server's internal mail binary to send messages. No additional configuration is required.

docs.title: 🚀 SMTP Server
docs.text: Recommended for high-deliverability requirements (e.g., using Gmail, SendGrid, or Mailgun). - SMTP Host: The address of your mail provider (e.g., smtp.gmail.com). - SMTP Port: Usually 465 (SSL/TLS) or 587 (STARTTLS). - Authentication: Input your full email address and the corresponding password or App Password.

docs.title: 🔗 Permalinks (Smart URL)
docs.text:
The Permalink system allows you to transform complex PHP queries into clean, SEO-friendly paths.

  • Standard: yoursite.com/index.php?post=123
  • Smart URL: yoursite.com/my-awesome-post.html

[!IMPORTANT] Server Config: Enabling Smart URLs requires mod_rewrite on Apache (handled via .htaccess) or specific location blocks on Nginx. If your site returns a 404 after enabling this, check your server's rewrite engine status.

docs.title: 🎨 Branding (Logo & Favicon)
docs.text:
Personalize your site's visual identity.

  • Primary Logo: Upload a high-quality (but optimized) image or provide a direct external URL.
  • Favicon: The small icon appearing in browser tabs. Always provide a full, absolute URL to avoid path resolution issues during routing.
  • Logo Mode: Switch between displaying the Image Logo or the Text-based Site Name.

docs.title: 📚 Library & Frontend CDN
docs.text:
GeniXCMS relies on several industry-standard frontend libraries. You can manage their loading behavior here.

  • jQuery: Enter the specific version number (e.g., 3.7.1) to load from a global CDN.
  • FontAwesome: Enable or disable the FontAwesome icon stack.
